coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: A low-temperature trypsin-assisted preparation and Fe3+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Jie
Chen, Yonglei
Han, Yangxia
Liu, Juanjuan
Ren, Cuiling
Chen, Xingguo
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, extensive researches are focused on the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) due to their excellent photochemical, biocompatible and water-soluble properties. However, these synthesis methods are generally suffered from tedious processes. In this paper, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are synthesized by a facile, one-pot, low-temperature method with trypsin and dopamine as precursors. The synthesis process avoids any heating operation and organic solvent, which provides a “green” and effective preparation route. The obtained CNPs exhibit excellent water-solubility, salt-tolerance and photostability. Based on the synergistic action of the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism, the CNPs are exploited as a “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. The probe shows a wide linear range from 0.1 to 500 μM, with a limit of detection of 30 nM. Furthermore, the as-fabricated fluorescent sensing system is successfully applied to the analysis of Fe3+ in biological samples such as human urine and serum samples with satisfactory recoveries (92.8–113.3%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Low-temperature synthesis
Dopamine
Trypsin
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield
Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ based on the CNPs probe
Possible mechanism of the FL response of the CNPs to Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (black curve) and fluorescent emission spectra (red curve) of the CNPs under excitation wavelength at 317 nm. (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of the CNPs at various excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) TEM image of the CNPs (scale bar 20 nm). Inset is the SAED pattern of the CNPs. (B) The size distribution histogram of the CNPs. (C) AFM image of the CNPs deposited on a mica substrate. (D) The height profile along the line in (C). (E) Powder XRD pattern of the CNPs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of products prepared by using various proteases react with DA at room temperature. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the CNPs prepared by using nature, heating-denatured and urea-denatured trypsin to react with DA. (C) Far-UV circular dichroism spectra of the CNPs and nature trypsin. (D) Normalized FL intensity of the CNPs synthesized under different reaction temperatures.

(A) FT-IR spectra of the CNPs (black curve), PDA (red curve) and DA (blue curve). High resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, and (D) O 1s peak of the CNPs, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) The comparison of mass percentage of C, N, and O in the CNPs and PDA. (B) TGA curve of the CNPs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CNPs after storage for 5 h and 1 month at room temperature, respectively. (B) FL intensity variation of the CNPs as a function of time under 317 nm light illumination. (C) Normalized FL intensity of the CNPs in pH 7.4 and 5.0 solutions containing various concentrations of NaCl. (D) Fluorescence spectra of the CNPs after incubation at different temperatures for 10 min (interval, 10 °C). (E) Reversible FL intensity of the CNPs nanoswitch through changing temperature. (F) Normalized FL intensity of the CNPs at different pH media.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CNPs upon the addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom, 0, 0.1, 0.5, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 μM). Inset is the photographs of the CNPs solutions in the absence and presence of 500 μM Fe3+ under UV light (365 nm) illumination and the linear calibration plot between F 0/F and the concentration of Fe3+ (0.1–500 μM). The fitted linear data can be expressed as the Stern–Volmer equation F 0/F = 1.01 + 0.003[Fe3+]. Error bars are the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (B) FL intensity response (F/F 0) of the CNPs toward various metal ions (black bars) and the subsequent addition of Fe3+ (red bars). (C) FL intensity response (F/F 0) of the CNPs toward various amino acids and other interferents (from left to right: blank, tryptophane, histidine, threonine, lysine, glycine, serine, cysteine, alanine, citric acid, oxalic acid, glutathione, and Fe3+). The concentration of Fe3+ and interferents are 1.0 mM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CNPs in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of 200 μM of Fe3+. Inset is the UV–vis spectra of the CNPs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from bottom to top, 0, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400, 500 μM). (B) Suppressed efficiency (E, %) of observed (black line, E obsd) and corrected (red line, E cor) fluorescence intensity, with removal of IFE from E obsd measurements for the CNPs after each addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A). Stern–Volmer plots describing quenching of FL intensity and lifetimes of the CNPs by various concentrations of Fe3+. (B). Time-resolved decays of the CNPs in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of 500 μM of Fe3+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The synthetic strategy of the CNPs.
Comparison of the present approach with other reported methods for the detection of Fe3+.
Analytical results for the detection of Fe3+ in different natural samples by the proposed method and ICP-AES method.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of the CNPs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: A ex is the absorbance of the CNPs upon addition of Fe3+ at 317 nm.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: A em is the absorbance of the CNPs upon addition of Fe3+ at 490 nm.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Corrected factor (CF) is calculated as F cor/F obsd.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: F obsd is the measured FL intensity of the CNPs upon addition of Fe3+ at 490 nm.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: F cor is the corrected FL intensity with Eq. (4) by removing IFE from the measured FL intensity (F obsd).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: E obsd = 1 − F obsd/F obsd,0, in which F obsd,0 is the observed FL intensities of the CNPs in the absence of Fe3+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: E cor = 1 − F cor/F cor,0, in which F cor,0 is the corrected FL intensities of the CNPs in the absence of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: A low-temperature trypsin-assisted preparation and Fe3+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu, China.
Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, extensive researches are focused on the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) due to their excellent photochemical, biocompatible and water-soluble properties. However, these synthesis methods are generally suffered from tedious processes. In this paper, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are synthesized by a facile, one-pot, low-temperature method with trypsin and dopamine as precursors. The synthesis process avoids any heating operation and organic solvent, which provides a “green” and effective preparation route. The obtained CNPs exhibit excellent water-solubility, salt-tolerance and photostability. Based on the synergistic action of the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism, the CNPs are exploited as a “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. The probe shows a wide linear range from 0.1 to 500 μM, with a limit of detection of 30 nM. Furthermore, the as-fabricated fluorescent sensing system is successfully applied to the analysis of Fe3+ in biological samples such as human urine and serum samples with satisfactory recoveries (92.8–113.3%).

ce:section.ce:para: In decades, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), compared with traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, have attracted considerable attentions due to their specific advantages such as excellent aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity, easy functionality and high fluorescent activity [1–5]. Carbon nanoparticles are promising scaffolds for applications in photocatalysis, energy conversion, biosensor, and bioimaging [6,7]. As a result, extensive efforts have been paid on the synthesis of these benign fluorescent carbon materials. Common routes in preparing fluorescent CNPs include etching of large carbon structure, electrochemical oxidation, pyrolysis and carbonization of carbonaceous compounds, etc [8,9]. However, most of these methods involve tedious processes such as harsh synthetic conditions, requirement of high temperature or microwave-assisted devices and use of organic solvents. These factors limit the availability of the CNPs for practical applications. Hence, exploring a simple, rapid, and eco-friendly method to synthesize fluorescent CNPs still remains great challenge.

Recently, protein-directed synthesis of nanomaterials have gained increasing interest because the processes provide a simple, green, and effective route. The amine, carboxyl, and thiol groups in these molecules can serve as effective stabilizing agents in such formulations [10,11]. Trypsin, a common commercially available protease, is reported to act as linking and reducing agents for synthesis of gold nanoparticles-nanoclusters under a mild condition [12]. Nevertheless, thus far, the use of trypsin as stabilizer for the synthesis of fluorescent CNPs has never been reported.

Iron is one of essential trace elements and a necessity for many physiological processes in human and animal bodies. Iron ions (Fe3+) in body play essential roles in oxygen uptake, oxygen metabolism and electron transfer, and so on [13]. The presence of Fe3+ in biological systems has to be moderated as both its deficiency and overloading can impair cellular functions and induce various biological disorders [14]. Therefore, determination of Fe3+ is of great importance in health monitoring.

Herein, a facile, effective, one-pot synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent CNPs at a low temperature is established. Trypsin, based on the existence of tyrosine (Tyr) residues and inner space protection effect, is employed to provide an effective scaffold for the formation of the CNPs (Scheme 1 ). Dopamine (DA) is used as the carbon source. The whole preparation strategy is conducted in aqueous condition at room temperature. It avoids the requirement of toxic organic solvents and heating operation, and simplifies the synthesis conditions compared with previous reported hydrothermal and solid phase methods. The as-prepared fluorescent CNPs emit a blue-green fluorescence with a quantum yield of about 5.8% and exhibit excellent photostability and water-solubility. Furthermore, it is found that the fluorescence of the as-prepared CNPs can be quenched when Fe3+ are introduced into the solution. Hence, a “turn-off” sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ is developed. The detection mechanism is based on the synergistic action of inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism induced fluorescent quenching of the CNPs. This method is successfully applied for detection of Fe3+ in biological samples such as human urine and serum samples with good spiked recoveries.

Dopamine hydrochloride is purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. Trypsin from bovine pancreas is obtained from Aladdin Co. Pepsin, papain, bromelain and lysozyme are all purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. All other reagents are of analytical grade and used without any further purification. All stock and buffer solutions are prepared using deionized water in all experimental procedures.

The CNPs are prepared as follows: 40.0 mg of trypsin powder is added to 10.0 mL dopamine solution (1.0 mM) and the mixture is vigorously stirred for 5 min. Then pH of the mixture is adjusted to 11.0 by 1.0 M NaOH. The solution is stirred continuously for 5 h. After the reaction, the obtained CNPs are purified through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h. The whole experiments are conducted at room temperature. The as-prepared CNPs are freeze-dried till powder for further characterization. Control experiments are conducted as follows. 1.0 mM DA solution is adjusted to pH 11.0 by 1.0 M NaOH and stirred for 5 h at room temperature for preparation of the PDA (polydopamine). The transformed trypsin is obtained with the above synthetic method, in which trypsin is instead of DA.

To examine the effect of different protease as precursor on the synthesis of CNPs, equal concentrations of proteases such as pepsin, bromelain, papain, and lysozyme are reacted with 1.0 mM DA for analysis, under otherwise identical conditions for the preparation of the CNPs. In order to discuss the effect of the secondary structure of the trypsin on the formation of the CNPs, heat-denatured trypsin is prepared by incubating at 95 °C in a water bath for 15 min, and urea-denatured trypsin is obtained by pretreating with 8.0 M urea for 45 min. Then the denatured trypsin is reacted with DA.

To test the influence of reaction temperature on the synthesis of the CNPs, the mixture of DA and trypsin are stirred under different temperature conditions (0, 15, 25, 35, 45, 55, and 65 °C, respectively).

100 μL of the prepared CNPs stock solution is mixed with 3.0 mL of 2.0 mM PBS buffer solution (pH 5.0). 10.0 μL of different concentration of Fe3+ ions is added. The fluorescence emission spectra are recorded under excitation wavelength at 317 nm after 1 min. The selectivity of the CNPs probe toward Fe3+ ions is evaluated by adding other metal ions solutions or amino acids instead of Fe3+ ions in a similar way. All experiments are performed at room temperature.

Human urine and serum samples are supplied by the first affiliated hospital of Lanzhou University and the authors state that all experiments are performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. The institutional committee of the Lanzhou University approves the experiments. The authors also state that informed consent is obtained for any experimentation with human subjects and Lanzhou University is committed to the protection and safety of human subjects involved in the research. The urine and serum samples are subjected to a 50-fold and 100-fold dilution before analysis, respectively. Diluted samples are spiked with various concentrations of standard Fe3+ solution for further usage.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CNPs is obtained using a Tecnai G2F30 instrument. A drop of the CNPs solution is placed on a copper grid coated with a thin layer of amorphous carbon film and dried for the TEM analysis. Selected-area electron-diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CNPs is recorded on the same instrument. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image is recorded in the tapping mode with an Agilent 5400 (USA) instrument. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) are conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer using KBr pellets. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is performed on a D/max 82400 X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154056 Å). Thermogravimetry (TGA) curve of the CNPs is obtained by a STA PT1600 Thermal Analyzer with protection in nitrogen atmosphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement is performed using a PerkinElmer PHI-5702 multifunctional photoelectron spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα exciting source. Zeta potentials of the CNPs in different pH solutions are recorded by a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). Far-UV circular dichroism spectra of trypsin and CNPs prepared with different reaction conditions are recorded at 25 °C on Olis DSM 1000 double beam spectrophotometer. UV–visible absorption spectra are recorded by a TU-1901 double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence measurements are carried out using a RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer with both excitation and emission slit set at 10.0 nm. The excitation wavelength is set at 317 nm. Samples for absorption and emission measurements are conducted in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cuvette.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield of the CNPs is measured by the method mentioned in our previous work [15]. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield can be represented simple in the equation below:
(1)
QY =
∫
L
emission
∫
E
solvent
−
∫
E
sample

Here, QY is the absolute quantum yield, L emission is the fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum of the CNPs sample, collected using the sphere; E sample is the spectrum of the light used to excite the sample, collected using the sphere; E solvent is the spectrum of the light used for excitation with only the solvent in the sphere, collected using the sphere. The solvent in this experiment is deionized water.

Time-resolved fluorescence spectra are carried out in a time-correlated-single-photo-counting (TCSPC) system from FL 920 spectrometer with λex = 330 nm. The decay traces for the CNPs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ are fitted using biexponential functions Y(t) based on non-linear least squares analysis in Eq. (2) [16].
(2)
Y
( t )
=
α 1
exp
( − t /
τ 1
)
+
α 2
exp
( − t /
τ 2
)
where α 1 and α 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2. According to Eq. (3) [17],
(3)
τ ¯
=
α 1
τ 1 2
+
α 2
τ 2 2
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2
the average lifetimes
(
τ ¯
)
of the CNPs under different conditions are calculated.

In a typical experiment, trypsin powder is added to the DA solution under vigorous stirring. Five minutes later, the mixture is adjusted to pH 11.0 by 1.0 M NaOH and continuously stirred at room temperature for 5 h. The color of the solution changes from colorless to light yellow, and then to light brown, which implies the formation of the CNPs. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the prepared CNPs are studied. An absorption peak at 279 nm is observed in the UV–vis spectra of the CNPs (Fig. 1 A, black curve), which is assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [18]. In addition, there is a broad absorption band around 365 nm, corresponding to the transition of n-π* of the CNPs [19]. Red curve in Fig. 1A is the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CNPs, which shows a strongest emission peak centered at 490 nm under excitation of 317 nm. A typical excitation-dependent emission behavior of the CNPs is also observed (Fig. 1B), as in the case of most fluorescent carbon materials [20,21]. This behavior is attributed to the optical selection of different-size nanoparticles (quantum effect) and different surface state affecting the band gap of CNPs [22–24]. It is well-known that DA can self-polymerize under alkaline conditions to generate PDA [25]. Zhang et al. and Adem Yildirim have been reported that PDA nanoparticles are fluorescent and can be used for cellular imaging and dopamine sensing [26,27]. Trypsin solution itself can also emit a blue fluorescence peak centered at 339 nm when excited at 280 nm, which comes from tryptophan (Trp) and Tyr residues [28]. Hence, control experiments are conducted in order to exclude the possibility that the observed fluorescence is from a transformed trypsin or PDA. As shown in Fig. S1, none of these products show fluorescence emissions under the excitation of 317 nm, which confirms that the fluorescence emission centered at 490 nm is ascribed to the CNPs. To further confirm the formation of the CNPs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis is carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the as-prepared CNPs are monodispersed and approximately spherical in shape with an average diameter of 4.0–5.0 nm. The atomic force microscopic (AFM) image with associated height analysis (Fig. 2C and D) is in accordance with the above results. From the high-resolution TEM image, most particles are observed to be amorphous carbon particles without any lattices. It is also evidenced from the diffuse ring in the SAED pattern (inset in Fig. 2A) [29]. And the XRD pattern of the CNPs shows that there is a broad (002) peak centered at 3.4 Å, which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms in the as-prepared CNPs (Fig. 2E) [30].

To investigate the role of trypsin in the formation of the CNPs, several other proteases (i.e., pepsin, bromelain, papain, and lysozyme) are employed instead of trypsin for synthesis of the CNPs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, in the presence of pepsin, bromelain, papain and lysozyme, very weak fluorescence emissions at 490 nm are observed, indicating that only trypsin is effective in the formation of the CNPs. Although it is not clear at the molecular level how trypsin “biomineralized” the fluorescent CNPs, several revealing observations are performed as follows. It has been reported that Tyr residues or custom peptides containing Tyr residues possess great reduction capability when the reaction pH is adjusted above the pK a of Tyr (∼10) [11,31]. As trypsin contains 7 cysteine (Cys) and 10 Tyr residues, and the CNPs exhibit highest fluorescence (FL) intensity when the initial pH of the mixture is 11.0 in this study (Fig. S2), we suspect the Tyr residues in the trypsin may play an important role in the formation of the CNPs. To certify this assumption, the sole tyrosine, cysteine, and the mixture of the two amino acids are performed to react with DA solution respectively, under otherwise identical conditions for the preparation of the CNPs. The fluorescence properties of the obtained products are investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, in the presence of tyrosine, a similar to but weaker emission peak is observed (curve b in Fig. S3). While no fluorescence signals are caught when DA is reacted with cysteine or the mixture of cysteine and tyrosine (curve d and e in Fig. S3). Thus, it is concluded that the Tyr residues have a positive effect in the process of synthesis of the CNPs. It's worth noting that although Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) contains 21 Tyr residues, much weaker fluorescence signal compared with that of the CNPs is observed when BSA is used as precursor (curve c in Fig. S3). Therefore, it can be deduced that not only the existence of Tyr residues in the trypsin but also the steric protection due to the bulkiness of the trypsin provides facilities for the formation of the CNPs. To confirm this conclusion, we further, respectively, adopt heating-denatured and urea-denatured trypsin to study the mechanism of the CNPs formation. As shown in Fig. 3B, when DA is reacted with heating-denatured or urea-denatured trypsin, the FL intensity is weakened. Taken together, the results indicate that only trypsin with regular conformational behavior can effectively stabilize the fluorescent CNPs. The circular dichroism (CD) spectrum method is employed to study the conformational behavior of trypsin before and after the formation of the CNPs [32]. The 208 nm peak present in pure trypsin is shifted to 204 nm after the formation of the CNPs (Fig. 3C), indicating the loss of α-helix content and increase in β-sheet and random coil structures.

Generally, preparations of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by previous hydrothermal and solid-phase synthesis methods are performed under heating operation [33–36]. However, in this study it is worth noting that the CNPs prepared under higher temperature conditions show weaker FL intensities, while the CNPs synthesized at 0 °C exhibit the maximum FL intensity (Fig. 3D). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CNPs synthesized at 0 °C is ∼5.8%. The reason of low-temperature directed synthesis process may be as follows. Under high temperature condition, DA is spontaneously oxidized to its quinone derivative and subsequent self-polymerization of DA plays a major role in the solution; while under low temperature, the formation of PDA is suppressed and trypsin-directed formation of the CNPs proceeds to the greater degree. This hypothesis is supported by the UV–vis spectra of the prepared CNPs (Fig. S4). With the increase of the reaction temperature, the absorption peak around 390 nm is developed due to the formation of the quinone, together with increasing absorption between 400 and 700 nm, which shows the formation of PDA under high temperature [27,37,38]. In addition, it is well known that the secondary structure of protein is partial perturbed under high temperature. Thus, the steric protection of trypsin in the formation of the CNPs is weaken, which may be another reason why the FL intensities of the CNPs prepared under high temperatures are decreased. Accordingly, the synthesis route for preparation of the CNPs is favorable at room temperature or even lower temperature. The whole process is accomplished within 5 h (Fig. S5). Taking the above results together, the proposed low-temperature trypsin-assisted preparing of CNPs is conducted under mild conditions and it avoids the high temperature and high pressure operation, which offers a facile, effective, and “green” route for synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. In the following characterization, the CNPs prepared at room temperature are chosen as the model.

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4 A) are used to identify the surface functional groups present on the as-prepared CNPs. Broad absorption bands at 3100–3500 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH [30], and the band at 1650 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of CO, and absorption band at 1540 cm−1 is the characteristic stretching vibrations of CC [39]. Bands from 1049 to 1090 cm−1 are attributed to the COH stretching vibrations [19]. In addition, absorption band at 1400 cm−1 is related to CNH bending vibration, which suggests that nitrogen atoms are successfully doped into the CNPs [35,40]. These results indicate that there are abundant of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of the CNPs. The functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the CNPs in aqueous system, which is conducive to the potential applications of CNPs in the field of fluorescence probes. The surface composition and elemental contents for the CNPs are further determined by XPS. In the XPS broad spectrum of the CNPs, four strong peaks at 285.2, 399.2, 531.2 and 164.2 eV are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p, respectively (Fig. S6). The peak at 496.2 eV is attributed to Na KLL which comes from the NaOH. Limited amount of Cl element is due to the salt of dopamine hydrochloride precursor. The high resolution C 1s spectrum of the CNPs suggests the presence of CC/CC (284.5 eV), CN (285.7 eV), CO (286.7 eV), and CO (287.8 eV) groups, respectively (Fig. 4B) [41]. The XPS spectrum of N 1s exhibits two fitted peaks at 399.5 and 400.6 eV, which are ascribed to CNC and N(C)3 groups, respectively (Fig. 4C) [21]. The two fitted peaks at 531.0 and 532.3 eV in O 1s spectrum confirm the presence of CO and COH/COC groups, respectively (Fig. 4D) [42]. The surface components of the CNPs conducted by XPS are consistent with the FTIR results. On the basis of the peak intensities of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, the concentration of nitrogen content in CNPs is calculated to be 24.1%, which is much higher than that in PDA (2.5%) (Fig. 5 A). It further confirms that N atoms have been successfully incorporated into the framework of the CNPs. TG analysis (Fig. 5B) in the protection of nitrogen atmosphere shows a weight loss of 6.9% at T < 100 °C attributed to the loss of water molecules and a second weight loss of 16.5% at 254 °C due to the decomposition of the surface functional groups. Afterwards a third step at T > 300°C is attributed to the combustion of the carbon-based core [43].

The obtained CNPs in this method are observed to be very stable that the FL emission remains unchanged after one month storage (Fig. 6 A) or under continuous irradiation for 60 min (Fig. 6B). In addition, the CNPs display stable FL intensity even under the concentration of NaCl as high as 100 mM in the solution (Fig. 6C), showing excellent photostability and salt-tolerance of the CNPs. Moreover, temperature-dependent fluorescence of the CNPs is monitored. It is clear that the FL intensities of the CNPs solution is decreased gradually with the increase of temperature (Fig. 6D), which may be caused by the perturbation of protein in the CNPs at higher temperature [44]. And the FL intensity of the CNPs displays reversible changes by alternating temperature due to physical state changes (Fig. 6E). Furthermore, the effect of the pH on the fluorescence of the CNPs is also investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 6F that the FL intensity of the CNPs is weak under acid condition and remains nearly constant at pH 4.0–7.4, then it increases significantly when pH > 9.0. This pH-dependent fluorescence property can be understood in term of the change in surface charge owing to protonation-deprotonation [23], which is proved by the pH dependence of the zeta potentials of the CNPs (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ based on the CNPs probe
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, we find that the fluorescence of the CNPs can be effectively quenched when Fe3+ are added into the solution (Inset in Fig. 7 A). The reaction is completed within 1 min (Fig. S8). A significant linear correlation (R2 = 0.998) exists between the quenching efficiency (F 0/F) and the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.1–500 μM (Inset in Fig. 7A). The limit of detection (3s/k, in which s is the standard deviation for the blank solution, and k is the slope of the calibration curve) is estimated to be 30 nM, which is lower than those of many other methods shown in Table 1
[15,45–49]. Next, to investigate the selectivity of the CNPs probe toward Fe3+ ions sensing, potential interferences are tested such as other metal ions (including Mn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, and Hg2+ ions) and amino acids, etc. As shown in Fig. 7B and C, under the same test conditions, no obvious FL quenching is observed for these interferences. Meanwhile, the anti-interference performance of the sensing system is also evaluated by adding a mixture consisting of equal concentrations of Fe3+ and other potential metal ions into the sensing system. Little influence of the FL intensity is observed compared to that of the system in which only Fe3+ exist. These results reveal that the CNPs probe presents an excellent selectivity and strong tolerance for Fe3+ sensing. To evaluate its practical application, the CNPs-based sensor is used for Fe3+ detection in human urine and serum samples. The recoveries of this method are determined by spiking a series of different known concentrations of Fe3+. As shown in Table 2 , good recoveries are obtained, and the results obtained by the proposed assay are clearly in agreement with those obtained by the ICP-AES method. These results confirm the reliability and accuracy of the CNPs-based biosensor for monitoring Fe3+ in biological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the FL response of the CNPs to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the quenching mechanism of Fe3+, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ and the CNPs are investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the absorption peak of Fe3+ has a large overlapping with that of the CNPs, and the absorbance intensity is increased with the increase of Fe3+ concentrations (Inset in Fig. 8A). It suggests that the Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching of the CNPs may arise from the inner filter effect (IFE). In order to confirm this hypothesis, corrections are made by taking into consideration of the cuvette geometry used in the fluorescent measurements and absorption characteristics of the aqueous solution of the CNPs and Fe3+ with Eq. (4) [50,51].
(4)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 −
10
− d A ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 −
10
− s
A
em

Where, F obsd is the observed maximum FL intensity and F cor is the corrected maximum FL intensity after removing IFE from F obsd; A ex and A em are the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength (λ ex = 317 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λ em = 490 nm), respectively; As presented in Fig. S9, s is the thickness of the excitation beam (s = 0.10 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of cuvette (g = 0.40 cm), and d is the width of the cuvette (d = 1.00 cm). The maximum value of the correction factor could not exceed 3; otherwise, the correction is not convincing. The correction factor (CF), i.e. F cor/F obsd of IFE at each concentration of Fe3+ is calculated according to Eq. (4). Table 3 summarizes the CF of IFE, the observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiencies (E obsd and E cor) after addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ into the CNPs solution, respectively. The results demonstrate that approximately 80% of the suppressed efficiency comes from the IFE (Fig. 8B).

After removing the IFE, the remaining quenching effect is investigated. In general, fluorescence can be quenched through dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or static quenching effect (SQE) or both simultaneously [52]. A fitted linear Stern–Volmer plot between the quenching efficiency of the CNPs and the concentration of Fe3+ in this study means that there is only one quenching mechanism (dynamic or static) in this system [53]. To differentiate the two quenching mechanisms, lifetime quenching efficiencies (τ 0/τ) of the CNPs under various concentrations of Fe3+ are investigated. τ 0 and τ are the fluorescence lifetime of the CNPs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Fe3+, respectively. Quenching is considered to by dynamic when F 0/F = τ 0/τ, while static quenching is characterized by large discrepancies between F 0/F and τ 0/τ [54]. As shown in Fig. 9 , no obvious lifetime quenching is observed after addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. According to the above results, both the IFE and SQE may be responsible for the fluorescence quenching of the CNPs by Fe3+.

In conclusion, an easy and one-step synthesis method has been developed for blue-green fluorescent CNPs using trypsin and DA as precursors. The synthesis process is completed at room temperature or even lower temperature, which is quite different from those reported hydrothermal and solid-phase synthesis methods for carbon nanoparticles. The as-prepared CNPs are water-soluble and remarkable stable against ionic strengths and light illumination. This study provides a possible way for preparation of other carbon nanoparticles. Moreover, a CNPs based fluorescence sensor for detection of Fe3+ is constructed based on the synergistic action of IFE and SQE. This method, with its simplicity, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, can be used as a promising tool for Fe3+ analysis in biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21375053, 21207057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.04.039.
